content:
t realize you don’t need to be the pillar anymore; you only need to be the ground. 

I am the quiet weight in the room. I am the one who knows that the cracks in the plaster aren't just age—they are the building’s way of exhaling. For twenty years, I have walked the Richmond, and now, I am less a person and more a part of the local physics. I am the silence that follows the fog rolling in from the
